trans-unit.source: Remote Sensing and Raster data theory 🛰️
trans-unit.target: Remote Sensing and Raster data theory 🛰️

trans-unit.source: 🚧This training platform and the entire content is under ⚠️construction⚠️ and may not be shared or published! 🚧
trans-unit.target: 🚧This training platform and the entire content is under ⚠️construction⚠️ and may not be shared or published! 🚧

trans-unit.source: What is Raster Data
trans-unit.target: What is Raster Data

trans-unit.source: Raster data is next to vector data a fundamental component of GIS and represents spatial information as a rectangular grid of cells or pixels that are each associated with a specific geographical information. Each cell contains a value representing a certain attribute, such as elevation, temperature or land cover within a specific area. Regarding their structure, spatial rasters are very similar the representation of optical information in a digital picture, the difference being that a spatial raster is accompanied by spatial information that connects the data to a particular location.
trans-unit.target: Raster data is next to vector data a fundamental component of GIS and represents spatial information as a rectangular grid of cells or pixels that are each associated with a specific geographical information. Each cell contains a value representing a certain attribute, such as elevation, temperature or land cover within a specific area. Regarding their structure, spatial rasters are very similar the representation of optical information in a digital picture, the difference being that a spatial raster is accompanied by spatial information that connects the data to a particular location.

trans-unit.source: The data format raster data is particularly useful for capturing continuous phenomena across a geographical area such as temperature or elevation that do not suit the visualization with vector geometries due to their continuous nature without clear boundaries.
trans-unit.target: The data format raster data is particularly useful for capturing continuous phenomena across a geographical area such as temperature or elevation that do not suit the visualization with vector geometries due to their continuous nature without clear boundaries.

trans-unit.source: In each raster cell information is stored with a value that can be interpreted in the context of the attribute/phenomenon the raster expresses, for example elevation in meters or colour intensity in case of an image.
trans-unit.target: In each raster cell information is stored with a value that can be interpreted in the context of the attribute/phenomenon the raster expresses, for example elevation in meters or colour intensity in case of an image.

trans-unit.source: Types of Raster Data
trans-unit.target: Types of Raster Data

trans-unit.source: Raster data can be distinguished in two two common types according to the type of information that is displayed: Continuous and categorical raster data.
trans-unit.target: Raster data can be distinguished in two two common types according to the type of information that is displayed: Continuous and categorical raster data.

trans-unit.source: Continuous raster data refers to datasets where the values assigned to each pixel represent continuous phenomena that vary smoothly across the area covered by the raster. Some examples of continuous rasters include:
trans-unit.target: Continuous raster data refers to datasets where the values assigned to each pixel represent continuous phenomena that vary smoothly across the area covered by the raster. Some examples of continuous rasters include:

trans-unit.source: **Precipitation Maps** displaying variable precipitation sums
trans-unit.target: **Precipitation Maps** displaying variable precipitation sums

trans-unit.source: **Digital Elevation Models** containing altitude values
trans-unit.target: **Digital Elevation Models** containing altitude values

trans-unit.source: **Spatially variable Indices** such as the Normalized Difference vegetation Index (NDVI)
trans-unit.target: **Spatially variable Indices** such as the Normalized Difference vegetation Index (NDVI)

trans-unit.source: **Discontinuous** rasters contain categorical data, where each pixel represents a discrete class value rather than a value on a continuous scale. The information in these types of raster is sometimes also suitable for the storage with vector data. Some examples of classified maps include:
trans-unit.target: **Discontinuous** rasters contain categorical data, where each pixel represents a discrete class value rather than a value on a continuous scale. The information in these types of raster is sometimes also suitable for the storage with vector data. Some examples of classified maps include:

trans-unit.source: **Landcover/Land Use Maps** with discrete classes
trans-unit.target: **Landcover/Land Use Maps** with discrete classes

trans-unit.source: **Tree height maps classified** as short, medium, and tall trees
trans-unit.target: **Tree height maps classified** as short, medium, and tall trees

trans-unit.source: **Risk maps** with multiple distinct risk classes
trans-unit.target: **Risk maps** with multiple distinct risk classes

trans-unit.source: Properties of Raster data
trans-unit.target: Properties of Raster data

trans-unit.source: Spatial Extent
trans-unit.target: Spatial Extent

trans-unit.source: The spatial extent of a raster refers to the geographical area covered by the grid of cells in the raster dataset. It defines the boundaries of the raster dataset in terms of its geographical coordinates. The spatial extent is typically described by the minimum and maximum values of the spatial coordinates in every geographical direction (e.g. minimum and maximum latitude and longitude) that encompass all pixels of the raster.
trans-unit.target: The spatial extent of a raster refers to the geographical area covered by the grid of cells in the raster dataset. It defines the boundaries of the raster dataset in terms of its geographical coordinates. The spatial extent is typically described by the minimum and maximum values of the spatial coordinates in every geographical direction (e.g. minimum and maximum latitude and longitude) that encompass all pixels of the raster.

trans-unit.source: Spatial Resolution
trans-unit.target: Spatial Resolution

trans-unit.source: The spatial resolution of a raster refers to the area represented by each individual pixel on the ground in the real world. It quantifies the level of detail of the spatial representation within the dataset. For example, a spatial resolution of 30 metres per pixel means that each pixel represents an area of 30x30 metres on the ground. High spatial resolution means smaller pixel sizes, resulting in finer detail and more accurate representation of spatial features within the dataset.
trans-unit.target: The spatial resolution of a raster refers to the area represented by each individual pixel on the ground in the real world. It quantifies the level of detail of the spatial representation within the dataset. For example, a spatial resolution of 30 metres per pixel means that each pixel represents an area of 30x30 metres on the ground. High spatial resolution means smaller pixel sizes, resulting in finer detail and more accurate representation of spatial features within the dataset.

trans-unit.source: Coordinate Reference Sytem (CRS)
trans-unit.target: Coordinate Reference Sytem (CRS)

trans-unit.source:
As with vector data, the Coordinate Reference System (CRS) of a raster dataset refers to the spatial reference framework used to define the geographic location and orientation of the raster on the Earth's surface (CRS WIKI link). It provides a standardised way of representing spatial coordinates and ensures that raster data can be accurately geolocated and integrated with other geo-spatial datasets.

The CRS includes a number of different parameters:
trans-unit.target:
As with vector data, the Coordinate Reference System (CRS) of a raster dataset refers to the spatial reference framework used to define the geographic location and orientation of the raster on the Earth's surface (CRS WIKI link). It provides a standardised way of representing spatial coordinates and ensures that raster data can be accurately geolocated and integrated with other geo-spatial datasets.

The CRS includes a number of different parameters:

trans-unit.source: **Coordinate system**: This defines how spatial coordinates are represented. Common coordinate systems include geographic (latitude and longitude) and projected (e.g. Universal Transverse Mercator - UTM).
trans-unit.target: **Coordinate system**: This defines how spatial coordinates are represented. Common coordinate systems include geographic (latitude and longitude) and projected (e.g. Universal Transverse Mercator - UTM).

trans-unit.source: **Datum**: The datum specifies the reference ellipsoid and geodetic parameters used to model the shape of the Earth. It ensures consistency of spatial measurements across different datasets (e.g. the WGS 84 ellipsoid).
trans-unit.target: **Datum**: The datum specifies the reference ellipsoid and geodetic parameters used to model the shape of the Earth. It ensures consistency of spatial measurements across different datasets (e.g. the WGS 84 ellipsoid).

trans-unit.source: **Unit**: The unit of measurement for spatial coordinates, such as metres (e.g. in UTM) or degrees in the case of geographical coordinates.
trans-unit.target: **Unit**: The unit of measurement for spatial coordinates, such as metres (e.g. in UTM) or degrees in the case of geographical coordinates.

trans-unit.source: **Origin**: The origin or reference point of the coordinate system, which may vary depending on the projection method used.
trans-unit.target: **Origin**: The origin or reference point of the coordinate system, which may vary depending on the projection method used.

trans-unit.source: Metadata
trans-unit.target: Metadata

trans-unit.source:
Metadata for raster data consists of descriptive information that provides context and details about the raster dataset. It includes information such as the dataset source, creation date, spatial extent, spatial resolution, coordinate reference system (CRS), data type, units, compression techniques, processing steps, accuracy ratings, and copyright/licensing information.

This metadata helps users to understand the content, origin, quality and appropriate use of raster data. It facilitates data discovery, evaluation and integration into geospatial workflows, ensuring that users can effectively interpret and use the data for their specific applications.
trans-unit.target:
Metadata for raster data consists of descriptive information that provides context and details about the raster dataset. It includes information such as the dataset source, creation date, spatial extent, spatial resolution, coordinate reference system (CRS), data type, units, compression techniques, processing steps, accuracy ratings, and copyright/licensing information.

This metadata helps users to understand the content, origin, quality and appropriate use of raster data. It facilitates data discovery, evaluation and integration into geospatial workflows, ensuring that users can effectively interpret and use the data for their specific applications.

trans-unit.source: File Format
trans-unit.target: File Format

trans-unit.source: There are multiple different file formats for storing an working with raster data. Following a short overview over the most common formats that you will likely come by as you work with spaptial rasters:
trans-unit.target: There are multiple different file formats for storing an working with raster data. Following a short overview over the most common formats that you will likely come by as you work with spaptial rasters:

trans-unit.source: **GeoTIFF (.tif/.tiff)**: Widely used file format, supports georeferencing and metadata.
trans-unit.target: **GeoTIFF (.tif/.tiff)**: Widely used file format, supports georeferencing and metadata.

trans-unit.source: **GeoPackage (.gpkg)**: Open format for storing geospatial data, supports raster and vec-tor.
trans-unit.target: **GeoPackage (.gpkg)**: Open format for storing geospatial data, supports raster and vec-tor.

trans-unit.source: **JPEG (.jpg/.jpeg) & PNG (.png)**: Common formats for images, lacks georeferencing.
trans-unit.target: **JPEG (.jpg/.jpeg) & PNG (.png)**: Common formats for images, lacks georeferencing.

trans-unit.source: **Esri Grid (.adf)**: Esri's raster format, used in ArcGIS, supports georeferncing amd metadata
trans-unit.target: **Esri Grid (.adf)**: Esri's raster format, used in ArcGIS, supports georeferncing amd metadata

trans-unit.source: **GeoPackage (.gpkg)**: Open format for storing geospatial data, supports raster and vec-tor.
trans-unit.target: **GeoPackage (.gpkg)**: Open format for storing geospatial data, supports raster and vec-tor.
